content.text:
AI agents are fundamentally different from AI copilots.

Copilots:
→ Human-in-the-loop
→ User sees every action
→ User approves decisions

Agents:
→ Autonomous execution
→ Multi-step reasoning
→ Independent data access

The security model must be different too.

Agent authorization isn't user authorization with extra steps.

It's delegated authority with constraints:

1. Scope Boundaries
→ What data can this agent access?
→ Not what can the user access

2. Action Limits
→ What operations are permitted?
→ Read-only? Write? Execute?

3. Time Constraints
→ How long is this authority valid?
→ Session-based or task-based?

4. Audit Requirements
→ What did the agent actually do?
→ Complete decision trace

If you're deploying AI agents, your permission model needs an upgrade.
content.headline: AI Agent Authorization: A Different Model

entries.notes: Week 1: Security Gap - Agent authorization intro

thread:
AI agents need a different security model than AI copilots.

Here's why the distinction matters 🧵

1/ Copilots have a human-in-the-loop.

User sees every action.
User approves decisions.
User is the authorization boundary.

2/ Agents operate autonomously.

Multi-step reasoning.
Independent data access.
Decisions made without human review.

3/ The question changes:

Copilot: "What can the user access?"
Agent: "What can THIS AGENT access ON BEHALF OF the user?"

Delegated authority with constraints.

4/ Agent authorization requires:
→ Scope boundaries (what data?)
→ Action limits (read? write?)
→ Time constraints (how long valid?)
→ Complete audit trail

Different problem. Different solution.

entries.notes: Week 1: Security Gap - Agent authorization thread

content.text:
Your AI agent made 47 retrieval decisions in the last 5 seconds.

Can you explain any of them?

This is the observability gap in agentic AI.

When agents:
→ Browse multiple data sources
→ Chain retrieval queries
→ Make autonomous decisions

Traditional logging shows: "Agent completed task."

It doesn't show:
→ Which documents were considered
→ Which were filtered by permissions
→ Which informed the final answer
→ Why certain paths were taken

For enterprise deployment, you need retrieval-level observability.

Not just "what did the agent output?"

But "how did the agent get there?"

Gateco logs every retrieval decision in the chain.

Every permission check. Every policy evaluation. Every filtering decision.

Agent reasoning should be auditable.
content.headline: The Observability Gap in Agentic AI
content.cta: See how Gateco enables agent observability

content.text:
Your AI agent made 47 retrieval decisions in 5 seconds.

Can you explain any of them?

"Agent completed task" isn't enterprise-grade observability.

You need retrieval-level logging:
→ Documents considered
→ Permissions checked
→ Filtering decisions
→ Reasoning paths

Least privilege for AI agents is harder than you think.

For humans:
→ Define role
→ Assign permissions
→ Review periodically

For agents:
→ What's the "role" of an agent that can do anything?
→ How do you scope permissions for emergent behavior?
→ How often is "periodically" when agents run continuously?

The challenge: agents don't fit traditional IAM models.

They're not users (no identity lifecycle).
They're not services (behavior varies by task).
They're not applications (scope changes dynamically).

The solution: task-scoped authorization.

→ Grant permissions for this specific task
→ Constrain to this specific scope
→ Expire after this specific duration
→ Audit every action within the grant

Agents need just-in-time, just-enough permissions.

Not static role assignments.
content.headline: Least Privilege for AI Agents

entries.notes: Week 1: Security Gap - Least privilege for agents

content.headline: Agent Authorization Poll

content.text:
Implementing agent authorization scopes in Gateco:

```python
from gateco import GatecoClient, AgentScope

client = GatecoClient(api_key="gco_...")

# Define scope for this agent task
scope = AgentScope(
    principal="user:alice@company.com",  # On behalf of
    resources=["project:phoenix/*"],       # Can access
    actions=["read"],                       # Read only
    expires_in=300,                         # 5 minutes
    audit_level="detailed"                  # Log everything
)

# Agent operates within scope
async with client.scoped_session(scope) as session:
    results = await session.retrieve(query)
    # All retrievals constrained to scope
    # All decisions logged
    # Scope expires automatically
```

Key properties:

→ Delegated: Agent acts on behalf of user
→ Constrained: Only specified resources
→ Time-limited: Automatic expiration
→ Audited: Every decision logged

Task-scoped authorization. Not open-ended permissions.
content.headline: Agent Authorization Scopes in Code

thread:
Task-scoped authorization for AI agents in Python 🧵

How to implement delegated, constrained, time-limited permissions.

1/ Define the scope:

scope = AgentScope(
  principal="user:alice",
  resources=["project:phoenix/*"],
  actions=["read"],
  expires_in=300
)

Agent acts on behalf of Alice, but only for Project Phoenix, read-only, for 5 minutes.

2/ Run within scope:

async with client.scoped_session(scope):
  results = await session.retrieve(query)

All retrievals constrained.
All decisions logged.
Scope expires automatically.

3/ What you get:

✓ Delegated authority (on behalf of user)
✓ Constrained access (specified resources only)
✓ Time-limited (automatic expiration)
✓ Full audit trail (every decision logged)

Task-scoped, not open-ended.

content.text:
Integrating Gateco with LangChain agents:

```python
from langchain.agents import AgentExecutor
from gateco.langchain import GatecoRetrieverTool

# Create permission-aware retrieval tool
retriever_tool = GatecoRetrieverTool(
    name="knowledge_search",
    description="Search internal knowledge base",
    gateco_client=client,
    user_context=get_current_user
)

# Add to agent tools
agent = AgentExecutor.from_agent_and_tools(
    agent=llm_agent,
    tools=[retriever_tool, ...],
)

# Agent retrieval is now permission-aware
result = agent.run("Find information about Project Phoenix")
```

What happens:

1. Agent decides to use knowledge_search
2. GatecoRetrieverTool intercepts
3. User context evaluated
4. Only authorized chunks returned
5. Decision logged

Same LangChain patterns. Permission-aware retrieval.
content.headline: Gateco + LangChain Agents

content.text:
LangChain + Gateco = permission-aware agents

1. Agent calls retrieval tool
2. Gateco intercepts
3. User context evaluated
4. Only authorized chunks return
5. Decision logged

Same LangChain patterns. Added security layer.

Docs: https://gateco.io/docs/langchain

Debugging agent retrieval decisions:

When an agent returns unexpected results, the debugging workflow:

1. Find the retrieval in audit logs
2. Replay with Access Simulator
3. Understand exactly what happened

Access Simulator for agents:

→ Impersonate the agent's delegated principal
→ Run the same query
→ See which chunks were considered
→ See which were filtered and why
→ Understand the policy path

Debug view shows:

```
Query: "Project Phoenix status"
Principal: user:alice (via agent:research-bot)
Scope: project:phoenix/*

Chunk phoenix-001: ALLOWED (project member)
Chunk phoenix-002: ALLOWED (project member)
Chunk hr-comp-003: DENIED (outside scope)
Chunk phoenix-004: DENIED (classification: confidential)

Returned: 2 chunks
Filtered: 2 chunks
```

No more "the agent did something weird."

Now: "here's exactly what happened and why."
content.headline: Debugging Agent Retrieval

content.text:
Debugging agent retrieval:

"The agent did something weird."

Becomes:

"Here's exactly what chunks were considered, which were filtered, and why."

Access Simulator turns agent behavior from mystery to observable.

Multi-agent systems and permission boundaries:

Scenario: Research agent calls analysis agent calls data agent.

Question: What permissions does each agent have?

Option 1: Permission inheritance
→ Each agent inherits from caller
→ Scope narrows down the chain
→ Simplest model

Option 2: Independent scopes
→ Each agent has its own permissions
→ Defined at deployment
→ More control, more complexity

Option 3: Task-based delegation
→ Caller grants specific scope to callee
→ Scope can't exceed caller's permissions
→ Explicit trust boundaries

Gateco supports all three patterns.

The right choice depends on your trust model.

Key principle: explicit is better than implicit.

Document your agent permission model.
content.headline: Multi-Agent Permission Boundaries

content.text:
AI agent compliance in regulated industries:

New challenge: Agents that autonomously access sensitive data.

Healthcare (HIPAA):
→ Agent accessing PHI must be logged
→ Minimum necessary standard applies
→ Can't exceed user's access rights
→ Breach notification if compromised

Finance (SOX, FINRA):
→ Trading information access must be segregated
→ Audit trail required for all data access
→ Supervisory requirements extend to agents

Legal (Ethics rules):
→ Attorney-client privilege boundaries
→ Conflict checks required
→ Matter isolation mandatory

Common requirement: Prove the agent only accessed what it should have.

Gateco provides:
→ Policy enforcement per industry requirement
→ Complete audit logs
→ Compliance reporting exports
→ Access Simulator for audit demonstration

Agent compliance isn't optional in regulated industries.

content.text:
The EU AI Act and retrieval systems:

Under the EU AI Act, high-risk AI systems have specific requirements:

→ Transparency: Users must know AI is being used
→ Data governance: Training data must be documented
→ Human oversight: Meaningful human control required
→ Accuracy: Systems must be fit for purpose
→ Robustness: Security and resilience requirements

For RAG systems, this means:

1. Document what data the system can access
2. Explain how retrieval decisions are made
3. Provide human override capability
4. Maintain audit trails
5. Ensure unauthorized access is prevented

Permission-aware retrieval directly supports:
→ Data governance (documented access boundaries)
→ Human oversight (policy visibility)
→ Audit trails (every decision logged)
→ Robustness (unauthorized access prevented)

Regulatory compliance is an architecture decision.

Building RAG in the EU? Plan for this now.
content.headline: EU AI Act and RAG Systems

content.text:
EU AI Act implications for RAG systems:

✓ Document what data can be accessed
✓ Explain retrieval decisions
✓ Provide human override
✓ Maintain audit trails
✓ Prevent unauthorized access

Regulatory compliance is an architecture decision.

SOC 2 controls for AI retrieval systems:

When auditors evaluate your AI system, they're looking at:

CC6.1 - Logical access controls
→ Can you demonstrate who can access what?
→ Are access rights documented and reviewed?
→ How are permissions changed?

CC6.2 - System operations
→ How is the system monitored?
→ Are anomalies detected and responded to?

CC6.3 - Change management
→ How are policy changes approved?
→ Is there an audit trail for changes?

CC7.2 - System monitoring
→ Are security events logged?
→ Can you reconstruct what happened?

Gateco directly supports:
→ Policy documentation (CC6.1)
→ Real-time monitoring dashboard (CC6.2)
→ Policy versioning with approval workflow (CC6.3)
→ Complete audit logs with SIEM export (CC7.2)

SOC 2 readiness isn't an afterthought.
content.headline: SOC 2 Controls for AI Retrieval

content.text:
SOC 2 for AI retrieval:

CC6.1 - Who can access what?
CC6.2 - How is it monitored?
CC6.3 - How are changes approved?
CC7.2 - Are events logged?

If your RAG system can't answer these, you have audit findings waiting.

Question for compliance and legal teams:

→ Access control policies?
→ Audit log retention?
→ Data classification mapping?
→ Incident response plans?

Curious what's becoming standard practice.
content.headline: AI Compliance Documentation Poll

content.text:
3 months of Gateco.

What we've learned building permission-aware retrieval:

1. The problem is real
→ Every enterprise RAG deployment hits the security question
→ Most teams discover it late in the process
→ Retrofitting is harder than building right

2. Patterns are emerging
→ Department isolation is the baseline
→ Project-based access is the next layer
→ Temporal policies (time-limited access) are growing

3. Agents change everything
→ Copilots with humans in the loop are manageable
→ Autonomous agents need explicit scopes
→ Multi-agent systems need clear trust boundaries

4. Compliance drives adoption
→ Regulated industries (healthcare, finance, legal) move first
→ "Prove it works" is the new standard
→ Audit logs are table stakes

Thank you to everyone building with Gateco.

Your feedback shapes what comes next.
content.headline: 3 Months of Gateco - What We've Learned

content.text:
3 months of Gateco.

What we've learned:

1. Every enterprise RAG hits the security question
2. Department isolation → project access → temporal policies
3. Agents need explicit scopes
4. Compliance drives adoption

Thank you for building with us 🙏

Feature spotlight: Temporal Access Policies

Now in Gateco: time-based access control for retrieval.

Use cases:

📅 Project windows
→ Consultant can access M&A docs only during engagement
→ Access expires automatically at end date

🔒 Sensitive periods
→ Block earnings data access during quiet period
→ Lift restrictions after announcement

⏰ Time-of-day restrictions
→ PII access only during business hours
→ Off-hours queries get filtered results

📊 Temporary permissions
→ Elevate access for specific investigation
→ Audit trail shows elevation and expiration

How it works:

```yaml
policy:
  effect: allow
  conditions:
    time:
      after: 2026-06-01
      before: 2026-06-30
      hours: 09:00-17:00
      timezone: America/New_York
```

No cron jobs. No manual revocation. No forgotten access.

Time-aware policies evaluated at query time.
content.headline: Temporal Access Policies

content.text:

📅 Project windows (access expires at end date)

⏰ Time-of-day (PII only business hours)

No cron jobs. No manual revocation.

Time-aware policies, evaluated at query time.
